critique.summary: The article reports on a new hardware hack called 'Bliss' that compromises the security of the Xbox One console, previously considered unhackable since its 2013 release. The hack uses a voltage glitching technique to bypass security at the silicon level, allowing unsigned code to run and access to protected system components.
mainPoints: The Xbox One was considered highly secure and unhackable since its 2013 launch.
Markus 'Doom' Gaasedelen demonstrated the 'Bliss' double voltage glitch hack at the RE//verse 2026 conference.
The hack targets the CPU voltage rail to bypass memory protection and boot ROM security.
This hardware-level exploit is unpatchable and allows loading unsigned code at all system levels, including the hypervisor and OS.
The hack could enable new possibilities for digital archivists and emulation efforts.
There is some debate about the presence of an ARM Cortex processor in the Xbox One, with clarifications that an ARM-based security processor is embedded within the AMD APU.
critique.truthfulness: The article is factually accurate based on available information about the Xbox One's architecture and the recent RE//verse 2026 presentation by Markus Gaasedelen. The Xbox One was indeed considered highly secure, and the voltage glitching technique is a known hardware attack method. The presence of an ARM Cortex-based security processor within the AMD APU aligns with AMD's disclosures about platform security processors, though some debate exists about the timeline of its inclusion. The article cites the conference presentation and includes technical details consistent with known hardware hacking methods, supporting its credibility.
considerations: The article does not discuss potential legal or ethical implications of exploiting this hack.
It does not address how widespread or practical the hack is for typical users versus specialized researchers.
There is no mention of Microsoft's response or plans to mitigate risks from this hardware vulnerability.
The impact on Xbox One successors or newer consoles is only briefly mentioned without detail.
The article does not explore the potential security risks posed to users if this exploit becomes widely available.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the recent hack of the Xbox One's 2013 hardware, which was previously considered 'unhackable.' Commenters analyze the technical complexity of the hack, the implications for security and ownership, and the broader notion that no device is truly unhackable, especially with physical access.

comments.author: mike_hearn
comments.text:
Amazing talk. Here's a quick writeup if you don't want to watch the full hour or don't have enough hardware knowledge to follow what Markus is talking about, as he goes very fast, in some cases too fast to even let you read the text on his slides. It's mandatory to use the pause key to understand the full details even if you have a deep understanding of every relevant technology, of which he explains none.

The Xbox uses a very advanced variant of the same technologies that also exist on smartphones, tablets and Secure Boot enabled PCs. When fully operational the Xbox security system prevents any unsigned code from running, keeps all code encrypted, proves to remote servers (Xbox Live) that it's a genuine device running in a secure state, and on this base you can build strong anti-piracy checks and block cheating.

The Xbox has several processors and what follows applies to the Platform Security Processor. When a computer starts up (any computer), the CPU begins execution in a state in which basically nothing works, including external communication and even RAM. Executions starts at a 'reset vector' mapped to a boot ROM i.e. the bytes are hard-wired into the silicon itself and can't be changed. The boot ROM then executes instructions to progressively enable more and more hardware, including things like activating RAM. Until that point the whole CPU executes out of its cache lines and can't use more memory than exists on-die.

Getting to the state where the Xbox can achieve all its security goals thus requires it to boot through a series of chained steps which incrementally bring the hardware online, and each step must verify the integrity of the next. The boot ROM is only 19kb of code and a few more kb of data, and can't do much beyond just activating RAM, the memory mapping unit (called MPU on the Xbox), and reading some more code out of writeable flash RAM. The code it reads from flash RAM is the second stage bootloader where much more work gets done, but from this second stage on it can be patched remotely by Microsoft. So if bugs are found there or in any later stage, it hardly matters because MS can issue a software update and detect remotely on Xbox Live servers if that upgrade was applied, so kicking out cheaters and pirates. The second stage boot loader in turn loads more code from disk, signature checks and decrypts it, sets up lots of software security schemes like hypervisors and so on, all the way up to the OS and the games.

Therefore to break Xbox security permanently you have to attack the boot ROM, because that's the only part that can't be changed via a software update. It's the keys to the kingdom and this is what Markus attacked. Attacking the boot ROM is very, very hard. The Xbox team were highly competent:

• Normally the bringup code would be written by the CPU or BIOS vendors but MS wrote it all in house themselves from scratch.

• The code isn't public and has never leaked. To obtain it, someone had to decode it visually by looking at the chip under a scanning electron microscope and map the atomic pictures to bits and then to bytes.

• Having the code barely helps because there are no bugs in it whatsoever.

So, the only way to manipulate it is to actually screw with the internals of the CPU itself by "glitching", meaning tampering with the power supply to the chip at exactly the right moment to corrupt the state of the internal electronics. Glitching a processor has semi-random effects and you don't control what happens exactly, but sometimes you can get lucky and the CPU will skip instructions. By creating a device that reboots the machine over and over again, glitching each time, you can wait until one of those attempts gets lucky and makes a tiny mistake in the execution process.

Glitching attacks predate the Xbox and were mostly used on smartcards until the Xbox 360, which was successfully attacked this way. So Microsoft knew all about them and added many mitigations, beyond "just" writing bug free code:

1. The boot ROM is full of randomized loops that do nothing but which are designed to make it hard to know where in the program the CPU has got to. Glitching requires near perfect timing and this makes it harder.

2. They hardware-disabled the usual status readouts that can be used to know where the program got up to and debug the boot process.

3. They hash-chain execution to catch cases where steps were skipped, even though that's impossible according to program logic.

4. They effectively use a little 'kernel' and run parts of the boot sequence as 'user mode' programs, so that if sensitive parts of the code are glitched they are limited in how badly they can tamper with the boot process.

And apparently there are even more mitigations added post-2013. Markus managed to bypass these by chaining two glitch attacks together, one which skipped past the code that turned on the MMU, which made it possible to break out of one of the the usermode 'processes' (not really a process) and into the 'kernel', and one which then was able to corrupt the CPU state during a memcpy operation, allowing him to take control of the CPU as it was copying the next stage from flash RAM.

If you can take control of the boot ROM execution then you can proceed to decrypt the next stage, skip the signature checks and from there do whatever you want in ways that can't be detected remotely - however, the fact that you're using a 2013 Phat device still can be.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a comprehensive and technically accurate overview of the Xbox One's security and the nature of the hack, supported by detailed knowledge of hardware and software security.

comments.author: charcircuit
comments.text:
It wasn't unhackable and decrypted versions of games already have been dumped. There was even a public exploit published years ago.

https://github.com/exploits-forsale/collateral-damage

What's new here is that this compromises the entire system security giving access to the highest privilege level.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly points out that previous exploits existed but emphasizes the significance of the new hack in compromising the highest privilege level, adding important context.

comments.author: autoexec
comments.text:
> Whether PC users, our core readership, will be interested in actually emulating Xbox One, looks unlikely. The 2013 system’s game library is largely overlapped in better quality on the PC platform.

And this explains why it's stayed unhacked so long. There was very little incentive to hack the system when the games are all playable on a PC. Pirates, cheaters, archivists, and hackers could just go there. Microsoft's best security measure was making something nobody cared enough about to hack in the first place
comments.analysis: The comment offers a plausible reason for the delay in hacking the Xbox One, focusing on market incentives rather than technical difficulty, which aligns with general understanding.

comments.author: au8er
comments.text: This just again shows that given enough time skill, and resources, any security is pointless if the attacker has physical access to the device.
comments.analysis: This is a well-known security principle that physical access often undermines security, making the comment accurate but more general than specific to the Xbox One hack.

comments.author: nxc18
comments.text:
I think it counts as effectively unhackable since it remained unhacked until five and a half years after its successor went on the market.

I wonder if, assuming they continue making Xbox, they find a way to mitigate this in the next generation.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a reasonable perspective on the relative security of the device based on the timeline of hacks, though 'effectively unhackable' is subjective.

comments.author: Jerrrrrrrry
comments.text:
Created a voltage drop that exactly occurred to be timed to the key comparison, then a spike at the continuation.

Irl noop and forced execution control flow to effectively return true.

B e a utiful
comments.analysis: The comment briefly and accurately describes the glitching technique used in the hack, showing technical understanding but lacking broader context.

comments.author: tencentshill
comments.text: Note this only affects the very first original 2013 "VCR" hardware. Newer revisions and variants are still unaffected.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly clarifies the scope of the hack, limiting it to early hardware versions, which is important for understanding the impact.

comments.author: JoeAltmaier
comments.text: Physical possession of a machine is pretty hard to make secure. It's a different level of secure, an order of magnitude less secure than remote attackers. This is expected?
comments.analysis: The comment reflects a fundamental security principle about physical access, reinforcing the difficulty of securing hardware against such attacks.

comments.author: jvillegasd
comments.text: Don't ever call a thing "unhackable", because every single human creation is imperfect
comments.analysis: A general but accurate caution against absolute claims in security, emphasizing the inevitability of vulnerabilities.

comments.author: Simulacra
comments.analysis: This comment echoes the same caution as others, reinforcing the consensus that no system is truly unhackable.

comments.author: aservus
comments.text: xbox is always trying to limit the users, when a person buys something, he clearly gets the ownership of the thing yet companies nowadays are trying really hard to sell some subscription while giving the illusion that the owner of the product is in control all the while keeping him in control. is there anyone else who feels the same way?
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a critical opinion on digital ownership and corporate control, relevant but more subjective and less technical.

comments.author: tetrisgm
comments.text: This is great news. Hopefully this opens the floodgates towards emulation and homebrew. Not that there are really any exclusives, but it would be interesting.
comments.analysis: This comment is optimistic about the hack's potential benefits but speculative about its impact on emulation and homebrew communities.

comments.author: stinmpy
comments.text: Marcus used to work for Microsoft,  in the MSRC. I wonder if he used insider knowledge for this hack.
comments.analysis: This is speculative without evidence; insider knowledge might help but the hack is explained by technical glitching rather than leaks.

comments.author: echelon_musk
comments.analysis: This comment is a brief expression of camaraderie with the hacker, adding no substantive information.

comments.author: lionkor
comments.text: Is there any better format article or writeup? I couldn't find anything.
comments.analysis: This is a request for more information rather than a contribution to the discussion.

comments.author: everyone
comments.text: It had those e-fuses in it right? *Seriously* it should be illegal to sell anything with those.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a strong opinion on e-fuses, which are known hardware security features, but the legal claim is subjective.
